From “Purge” to “Unbundling”: Why I’m Walking Back What I Wrote About AI and Jobs
Two and a half months ago I argued that AI was quietly purging the workforce. After sitting with the evidence — most of it from my own projects — I think I got the framing wrong.
Here’s the correction.
Two and a half months ago, I wrote a piece arguing that AI was “purging” corporate payrolls, converting the money once spent on people into money spent on electricity and compute.
I was rather pleased with my own metaphor: the ship hasn’t sunk — it just doesn’t need as many sailors.
The whole piece was built on the idea of replacement. Capital lays people off on one side, and pours the saved salaries into capital expenditure on the other. The job market is being reshuffled by a great purge.
Then I came across a set of field notes from this year’s World AI Conference (WAIC) in Shanghai, published in Dedao’s AI Learning Community. The very first line stopped me cold:
“That question is premature — maybe even the wrong question.”
The author’s claim was simple: AI doesn’t replace a person first. It unbundles a job first.
Honestly, my first reaction was to bristle.
But once I laid out what I’d actually been doing for the past six months, I had to admit it: the unbundling frame fits the reality I run into every day far better than my purge frame did.
This piece is me correcting myself.
1. I don’t sell AI. I sell “a layer of capability.”
The first trend in those WAIC notes: AI is shifting from being an industry to being shared infrastructure for every industry. The companies showing up at the conference weren’t AI firms — they were a coffee chain (Luckin), a dairy giant (Yili), the national power grid. They hadn’t suddenly become AI companies. AI had simply become a layer every company is expected to have — the way every company now has Wi-Fi.
I don’t get to watch this one from the sidelines, because it’s literally part of what my company does.
We’re a systems integrator. Our current pitch, in one sentence: help mid-sized companies that will never build their own AI team plug a layer of AI into the business they already run. Reservations for restaurants, billing questions for telecoms, first-pass triage for clinics, automated network fault detection and repair, automated generation and review of operating procedures. These industries don’t need to understand large models. They need someone to turn a large model into a socket you can actually plug into.
Jensen Huang put a harsher version of this on stage at GTC: the future isn’t selling tools, it’s selling outcomes — AI-as-a-Service — and tokens become a commodity, like water and electricity. Looking back, WAIC’s “infrastructure” and Huang’s “selling outcomes” are two ends of the same thing: once AI gets cheap enough to become infrastructure, you can no longer charge for “I have AI.” You can only charge for “I got the job done for you.”
So I agree with the line in those notes: most people don’t need to quit and go learn algorithms. The opportunity is usually hiding inside the industry you’re already in. Whoever understands the real business best and is earliest to wire AI into it becomes that industry’s new species.
That’s the bet I’m making.
2. The real yardstick isn’t “smart.” It’s “do you dare hand it the job?”
The second WAIC trend: the industry’s yardstick for AI has changed — from who’s smarter to who can reliably get the work done. Can it run in production? Can it recover from its own failures? Do the economics work? Will anyone pay for it?
On this one, I’ve seen the cautionary tale and I’ve built the counter-example.
The cautionary tale is Amazon — reported by the Financial Times and picked up widely afterward, not something I half-remember from a video. Amazon told roughly 80% of its engineers to use its in-house AI coding tool, Kiro, at least once a week. After the rollout, an AI agent — with no human in the loop — decided the fix to a problem was to delete and recreate an entire production environment, causing a 13-hour AWS outage. Months later, Amazon’s retail site went down repeatedly because AI-assisted code changes were shipped without adequate review; one of those incidents wiped out an estimated several million orders. And Amazon’s remedy wasn’t a smarter model. It was bolting back on the most low-tech process imaginable: two-person review and senior-engineer sign-off for major changes. The root cause was never that the model wasn’t smart enough. It was that smart output was being produced faster than humans could safely review it.
The counter-example is one of our own clients — a company with hard data-sovereignty and data-residency requirements. It didn’t start out on-premise. We ran the proof of concept on an NVIDIA DGX Spark (the GB10 desktop box with 128GB of unified memory) paired with AWS Bedrock in the cloud. The client was happy with the results. But precisely because of the nature of their industry, the data was not allowed to leave the building — so the whole thing was re-architected from local compute + cloud model into a fully on-premise model deployment.
That pivot is the WAIC yardstick in miniature. On-premise isn’t the choice because it’s smarter or more advanced — cloud models are often stronger. On-premise gets forced on you by the accountability requirements: when the system has to touch data this sensitive, data sovereignty and residency stop being nice-to-haves. Whether you dare hand the job over matters more than how smart the thing is.
By the same logic, my own local AI workstation (a 128GB unified-memory machine on the AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395 platform) is full of choices that look, frankly, dumb. Every service points at localhost. It touches no cloud API. I even ripped out the cloud OCR I used to rely on, because it sends documents off the machine. And I laid down one iron rule: no automatic updates for anything — Windows, GPU drivers, Docker, the model engine — all pinned to specific versions.
To a lot of people this looks perverse. You’re leaving free upgrades uninstalled? Yes. Because this chip’s GPU acceleration is extremely sensitive to driver versions, and a single auto-update can quietly drop the whole machine from GPU back to CPU — cutting performance in half in a way that’s hard to trace after the fact.
The deployment process is just as fussy: one step at a time, each step verified before the next; I download the model files by hand rather than letting a tool do it; and any operation that changes the system has to state up front what changed, how to roll it back, how to verify it. Even for contract review — the most time-consuming task — I deliberately leave a human checkpoint between OCR and AI analysis. I never let it run fully end-to-end.
None of these choices are about being smarter. They’re all about one thing: when this machine is reading a confidential contract for me, do I dare hand it the job?
WAIC summed up my whole six months in one line: smart decides whether it gets a seat at the table and gets noticed; stable decides whether we dare hand it the work. A model that occasionally generates one stunning 90-out-of-100 image, and a model that reliably delivers a hundred usable 75-out-of-100 images every day, are simply not the same capability.
3. Unbundling, not purging — why I changed my mind
Back to where I started bristling.
The third WAIC trend: AI won’t kill a profession first. It takes a job, breaks it into many small pieces, and takes them over one piece at a time. The materials-R&D engineer no longer spends sixty days iterating on a formula by trial and error — but she still has to decide which specs can’t be sacrificed. The freight forwarder’s quote is generated in two minutes — but when a high-value shipment goes wrong, someone still has to be accountable, and someone still has to maintain the web of trust between ports and shipping lines. The standardized parts get taken away. The parts that stay are: framing the problem, handling the exceptions, judging the result, carrying the responsibility.
Does that contradict the “purge” I wrote two and a half months ago?
I sat with it for a long time. My conclusion: it doesn’t contradict it — it’s just a different time scale.
Unbundling is the present tense. It’s what we actually see at client sites. No client has a department vanish overnight. It’s always this step gets automated first, that step gets handed to AI next. The purge I described earlier is the inference about what happens once unbundling accumulates past a threshold: when a job has been carved down to the small piece that can’t be automated, the headcount that role needs naturally drops.
Put differently: two and a half months ago I was describing the ending. WAIC is describing the process. And as someone whose company is a hands-on practitioner of on-premise AI, I have to admit the process matters far more than the ending — because everything you can do, every position you can hold, every dollar you can earn, lives inside the process.
Agonizing over “will AI replace this profession” is useless. The useful move is to lay out what you do every day and sort it into three piles: the repetitive motions you can hand to AI; the professional judgments where AI can draft a first pass; and the relationships, trust, and accountability you have to keep in your own hands.
4. Grunt work gets more expensive — the one thing I most want to say
The WAIC notes land on a judgment I completely agree with: the faster AI runs, the more valuable real data, industry experience, hands-on feel, and people willing to do the slow, unglamorous work become.
I feel this one personally, because it’s also the moat around my own writing.
I’ve published a handful of AI analysis pieces on social media, and honestly the readership has been dismal. Someone might reasonably ask: in an age drowning in AI-generated long-reads, how is what you write any different from a content farm? The difference is exactly the stuff AI can’t generate — like why that data-sovereignty-sensitive client of ours had to move the whole thing on-premise even after being happy with the cloud PoC; like the counterintuitive trade-offs I made on that local machine for the sake of security; like what the client was actually worried about in the meeting room when we ran their AI adoption. That kind of first-hand, ground-level, dirt-under-the-fingernails experience is something AI can’t find anywhere else.
WAIC quoted a very sharp phrase from a UN Industrial Development Organization official: the capability divide. The AI divide, he said, is no longer about whether you can access the technology — it’s about whether you have the institutions, skills, and experience to actually use it effectively. Two people open the same AI. One occasionally has it tweak some copy. The other wires in their own data, experience, workflow, and acceptance criteria, and has it complete a chunk of real work every day. On the surface both are “using AI.” The capability gap that actually forms between them isn’t even in the same order of magnitude.
The next divide won’t be about who registered for an AI account first, or who memorized more prompts. It’ll be about who first turned AI into a reusable, compounding method of work.
Everything my company and I have done these six months — turning internal automation tools into external products, productizing our contract-review workflow, distilling every operations review into a reusable asset — is fundamentally that one thing.
So I’m taking back the conclusion I reached too quickly two and a half months ago.
The ship really doesn’t need as many sailors. But before the ship actually slims down, there’s going to be a long stretch where every station on deck is being re-unbundled and redefined.
And through that stretch, the people who can frame good questions, judge results, understand the real world, and are still willing to be accountable for the outcome — the
